Hello, everyone. This is Roxanne Liu from Oakland, California. This video is a demonstration of the precision of robotic surgery. This is a patient with a small left direct inguinal hernia. You can see he is extremely thin. I find that in these patients who are very thin with very small hernias, there is essentially no room for error. The peritoneum is extremely thin, so it has to be handled with extreme gentleness. It's very easy to tear the peritoneum. And once the peritoneum is torn, there isn't any redundant tissue to patch the defect or make up for the loss of the peritoneum.

The surgeon carefully handles the extremely thin peritoneum to avoid tearing, as there is no redundant tissue available for repair. The laparoscopic approach reveals the entry into the retro rectus space, with minimal fat present, relying on areolar tissue for plane development.

The surgeon navigates the retro rectus space, carefully dissecting along the natural areolar plane. The thin peritoneum and attenuated posterior rectus sheath are visible, highlighting the delicate anatomy in this thin patient.

The surgeon navigates the retroactive space, following the natural areolar plane with careful retraction. The peritoneum is visibly thin, requiring precise manipulation under ten times magnification. Tiny blood vessels are cauterized to maintain hemostasis, ensuring clear visibility of the anatomical planes.
